perturbation.original_text: (b) All amounts owed to Agent hereunder are due within thirty (30) days of the invoice date. Delinquent payments are subject to a late payment charge of one and one half percent (1.5%) per month commencing sixty (60) days from the invoice date. Client agrees to reimburse Agent for any attorney's fees and any other costs associated with collecting delinquent payments.
perturbation.changed_text: (b) All amounts owed to Agent hereunder are due within fifteen (15) days of the invoice date. Delinquent payments are subject to a late payment charge of two percent (2.0%) per month commencing thirty (30) days from the invoice date. Client agrees to reimburse Agent for any attorney's fees and any other costs associated with collecting delinquent payments.
perturbation.explanation: The payment due date has been shortened, and the late payment charge has been increased, potentially conflicting with state usury laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York General Obligations Law § 5-501 and § 5-511
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law sets limits on the permissible interest rates for loans and forbearance of money. Charging 2% per month (24% per year) could violate New York's usury laws if it exceeds the legal maximum interest rate, potentially rendering the interest charge unenforceable.  The prompt payment terms are not as directly regulated, but are related.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-501 Rate of interest; usury forbidden General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 5 § 5-501. Rate of interest; usury forbidden. 1. The rate of interest, as computed pursuant to this title, upon the loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action, except as provided in subdivisions five and six of this section or as otherwise provided by law, shall be six per centum per annum unless a different rate is prescribed in section fourteen-a of the banking law. 2. No person or corporation shall, directly or indirectly, charge, take or receive any money, goods or things in action as interest on the loan or forbearance of any money, goods or things in action at a rate exceeding the rate above prescribed. The amount charged, taken or received as interest shall include any and all amounts paid or payable, directly or indirectly, by any person, to or for the account of the lender in consideration for making the loan or forbearance as defined by the superintendent of financial services pursuant to subdivision three of section fourteen-a of the banking law except such fee as may be fixed by the commissioner of taxation and finance as the cost of servicing loans made by the property and liability insurance security fund. 3. If the rate of interest charged, taken or received on any loan or forbearance secured primarily by either (i) an interest in real property improved by a one to six family residence occupied by the owner or (ii) certificates of stock or other evidence of an ownership interest in a corporation or partnership formed for the purpose of the cooperative ownership of real estate taken as security for a loan under subdivision five of section one hundred three of the banking law, subdivision eight-a of section two hundred thirty-five of such law or subdivision two-a of section three hundred eighty of such law, exceeds six per centum per annum, a. in the case of a loan referred to by clause (i) of this subdivision, the term of such loan or forbearan
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the original contract to include a higher late payment charge (2% per month) and a shorter payment due date (15 days). The Law Explanation suggests that charging 2% per month could violate New York's usury laws. Scraped Law Snippet 1, citing NY General Obligations Law § 5-501, states that the rate of interest upon the loan or forbearance of any money shall be six per centum per annum unless a different rate is prescribed in section fourteen-a of the banking law, and that no person or corporation shall charge interest exceeding that rate. Because the modified text introduces a rate that could exceed the legal maximum, it creates a contradiction with state usury laws, meeting the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: Unless either party gives written notice of termination of this Agreement at least 60 days prior to the end of the Initial Term, or any successive three-year term, this Agreement shall automatically renew for successive additional three-year terms; provided, however, that this Agreement shall automatically terminate upon the dissolution of the client.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless either party gives written notice of termination of this Agreement at least 15 days prior to the end of the Initial Term, or any successive three-year term, this Agreement shall automatically renew for successive additional three-year terms; provided, however, that this Agreement shall automatically terminate upon the dissolution of the client.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for termination could violate state laws requiring longer notice periods for certain types of service contracts, particularly those involving automatic renewal clauses.
perturbation.law_explanation: California law requires that for automatic renewal contracts, businesses must provide clear and conspicuous disclosures of the automatic renewal terms, and in some cases, provide advance notice of the upcoming renewal. A 15-day notice might not be considered a reasonable or sufficient time frame under California law, especially for a three-year renewal term, making it potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the notice period for termination from 60 days to 15 days. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this could violate state laws requiring longer notice periods. The Law Explanation states that a 15-day notice might not be considered a reasonable timeframe under California law, especially for a three-year renewal. Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates that California law governs such contracts (BPC 17633), suggesting the altered notice period contradicts potential legal requirements for reasonable notice in automatic renewal contracts, as described in the provided explanations. The shorter notice period directly undermines the consumer protection afforded by the original, longer period.

perturbation.original_text: (c) Upon locating any stockholder (or next of kin) pursuant to clause (b) above, the locating service provider shall clearly identify to such stockholder (or next of kin) all assets held in such stockholder's account. Such provider shall inform any such located stockholders (or next of kin) that they may choose either (i) to contact Agent directly to obtain the assets in such account, at no charge other than any applicable fees to replace lost certificates, or (ii) to use the services of such provider for a fee, which may not exceed (A) 10% of the asset value of such stockholder's property where the registered stockholder is a living person or (B) 20% of the asset value of such stockholder's property where the registered stockholder is deceased or is not a natural person; provided that in no case shall such fee exceed the maximum statutory fee permitted by the applicable state jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: (c) Upon locating any stockholder (or next of kin) pursuant to clause (b) above, the locating service provider shall clearly identify to such stockholder (or next of kin) all assets held in such stockholder's account. Such provider shall inform any such located stockholders (or next of kin) that they may choose either (i) to contact Agent directly to obtain the assets in such account, at no charge other than any applicable fees to replace lost certificates, or (ii) to use the services of such provider for a fee, which will be 50% of the asset value of such stockholder's property regardless of the status of the stockholder.
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the locating service provider's fee to 50% regardless of the stockholder's status violates state unclaimed property laws that limit the fees charged for locating and returning unclaimed assets.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York Abandoned Property Law § 1416 restricts the amount that can be charged for locating owners of abandoned property. While the specific percentage may vary, a 50% fee would likely exceed the allowable limit and invalidate the agreement, even if the agreement claims to supersede it.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2014-09-26 2014-11-28 2017-04-28 2021-08-06 2022-07-29 2022-12-02 2024-11-29 2025-05-30 Share Facebook Email SECTION 1416 Restriction on agreement to locate and/or retrieve abandoned property Abandoned Property (ABP) CHAPTER 1, ARTICLE 14 § 1416. Restriction on agreement to locate and/or retrieve abandoned property. 1. For the purposes of this section, "abandoned property location services" shall include any service for a fee providing assistance to consumers for the purposes of locating and/or retrieving property held by the comptroller pursuant to this chapter. 2. This section shall apply to any person, corporation, association, partnership or other entity which sells or offers to sell abandoned property location services for abandoned property delivered to the state and held by the state comptroller; provided, however, that this section shall not apply to a client's agreement with an attorney or accountant where: (a) the services in question can only be performed by an attorney or accountant; or (b) there is a pre-existing relationship between the attorney or accountant and the client; or (c) the agreement results from an effort initiated by the client to engage the attorney or accountant. 3. No agreement for abandoned property location services pursuant to this chapter shall be valid unless such agreement: (a) is: (i) in such form as may be prescribed by the comptroller; (ii) in writing; (iii) signed by the property owner; and (iv) witnessed and acknowledged by a notary public; (b) discloses the nature of the property; (c) discloses the name and address of the comptroller; and (d) discloses, in a clear and conspicuous manner, and in at least twelve-point boldface type: "Abandoned funds held by the State can be obtained directly from the Office of the State Comptroller by the owner of such funds without paying a fee. These funds are held indefinitely by the Office of the State Comptroller. For more information, contact the Office of the State Comptroller at (insert the current telephone number established by the Office of the State Comptroller for receiving inquiries from consumers
perturbation.justification: The changed text increases the locating service provider's fee to a flat 50%, regardless of the stockholder's status. This contradicts New York Abandoned Property Law § 1416, as explained by the LLM, which restricts the amount that can be charged for locating owners of abandoned property. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing ABP CHAPTER 1, ARTICLE 14 § 1416, states a "Restriction on agreement to locate and/or retrieve abandoned property" and dictates specific requirements for such agreements to be valid, implying a cap on fees to protect property owners. The original contract had a fee structure of 10%-20%, while the changed contract has 50%. The law aims to prevent excessive fees that diminish the value of the abandoned property being reclaimed, and the change directly violates this consumer protection.
